显然，Skylake处理器的质量保证非常糟糕，苹果公司决定必须做出这一飞跃。
弗朗索瓦·皮德诺尔(François Piednoël)在英特尔工作了20年，既是首席工程师，也是性能架构师。现在，他决定回答每个人都在问的问题：为什么苹果决定在Mac上从英特尔转向ARM处理器？
正如PCGamer报道的那样，Piednoël以一种相当不同寻常的方式给出了他的解释。他录制了一段10分钟的视频，一边玩飞行模拟器X-Plane，一边讨论苹果的决定。您可以观看下面的视频：
Piednoël认为，苹果之所以选择改用ARM，是因为英特尔的Skylake处理器。更具体地说，Skylake芯片的质量保证显然很糟糕，Piednoël(在Skylake时代还在英特尔工作)解释说，“Skylake的质量保证不仅仅是一个问题…。这是异常糟糕的。我们对天湖里的小事引用得太多了。基本上，我们在苹果的伙伴成为架构中问题的头号提交者。结果真的，真的很糟糕。当你的客户发现的错误几乎和你自己发现的一样多时，你就没有找到正确的地方。
苹果以想要尽可能多地控制其产品的制造流水线和供应链而闻名，所以你可以想象英特尔处理器的缺陷对该公司来说是多么令人沮丧，特别是如果是苹果的工程师为其做英特尔的测试工作的话。Piednoël认为情况如此糟糕，以至于成为了“转折点”，因此苹果决定追逐后来被称为“苹果硅”(Apple Silicon)的产品。
Skylake微架构最早出现在2015年，因此基于ARM的Mac硬件在苹果显然已经处于研发阶段几年了。只有这样，才能让如此巨大的转变得以接受。苹果不可能推出一款ARM MacBook Pro，而且在性能和兼容性上至少不能与英特尔版本相提并论。我们将在今年晚些时候了解Apple Silicon的表现，届时预计将出现第一款ARM Mac。
note_en:
Apparently the quality assurance of Skylake processors was so bad Apple decided it had to make the leap.
  François Piednoël spent 20 years working at Intel as both a Principal Engineer and Performance Architect. Now, he’s decided to answer the question everyone is asking: Why did Apple decide to switch from Intel to ARM processors for the Mac?
 As  PCGamer reports, Piednoël offered his explanation in a rather unusual way. He recorded a 10-minute video discussing Apple’s decision while playing the flight simulator X-Plane. You can watch the video below:
  Piednoël believes that Apple opted to switch to ARM because of Intel’s Skylake processors. More specifically, the quality assurance for Skylake chips was apparently terrible, with Piednoël (who was still at Intel during the Skylake days) explaining, “The quality assurance of Skylake was more than a problem … It was abnormally bad. We were getting way too much citing for little things inside Skylake. Basically our buddies at Apple became the number one filer of problems in the architecture. And that went really, really bad. When your customer starts finding almost as much bugs as you found yourself, you’re not leading into the right place.”
 Apple is well-known for wanting to control as much of the manufacturing pipeline and supply chain as possible for its products, so you can imagine how frustrating buggy Intel processors were for the company, especially if it was Apple’s engineers doing Intel’s testing work for it. Piednoël believes the situation was so bad it acted as “the inflection point” and Apple decided to pursue what has become known as Apple Silicon as a consequence.
 The Skylake microarchitecture first appeared in 2015, so ARM-based Mac hardware has clearly been in the research and development phase for a few years at Apple. It had to be in order to allow for such a huge shift to be embraced. Apple can’t launch an ARM MacBook Pro and not have it at least  equal the Intel version on performance and compatibility. We’ll find out how well Apple Silicon performance later this year when the  first ARM Mac is expected to appear.
title: 英特尔工程师解释苹果为什么改用ARM
title_en: Intel Engineer Explains Why Apple Switched to ARM

美国国家航空航天局(NASA)的肯尼迪航天中心现在已有近60年的历史-它于1962年7月1日正式成立，是一个独立于阿拉巴马州马歇尔太空飞行中心的实体。不久之后，施工开始了。
当时，维尔纳·冯·布劳恩(Wernher Von Braun)和他的德国科学家团队领导的发射运行局驻扎在马歇尔。但NASA的领导人意识到，他们需要在佛罗里达州卡纳维拉尔角空军基地旁边拥有自己的设施。因此，他们在附近的梅里特岛新建了一个发射运营中心。1963年11月约翰·F·肯尼迪总统在达拉斯遇刺一周后，林登·B·约翰逊总统将该设施重新命名为肯尼迪航天中心。
随着阿波罗计划的制定，美国国家航空航天局(NASA)也很快意识到，它需要一座大型建筑来组装土星V号火箭，为登月提供动力。工程开始于当时被称为垂直组装大楼(VAB)的地方，在那里，大型火箭将以垂直的形式堆放在一起，然后再向发射台推出。
这座160米高的建筑于1965年竣工，1966年竣工。VAB&#39；开发项目的施工照片--这座建筑需要近9万吨钢材--和完工后的建筑本身一样令人难以置信。但NASA官员当时想明确表示，这座建筑本身并不是终点。相反，这是一种达到目的的手段。
这座建筑不是纪念碑，1965年监督佛罗里达设施建设的德国火箭科学家库尔特·德布斯(Kurt Debus)说。它是一种工具，如果你愿意的话，它能够容纳重型运载火箭。因此，如果人们对它的庞大印象深刻，他们应该记住，在这种情况下，它的巨大是火箭动力运输系统的一个因素，它为美国提供了广泛的能力，可以在外层空间做任何国家目的。
甚至在它建成之前，VAB&#39；VAB&#39；的名字就从“垂直”更名为“运载工具组装大楼”，因为人们认为这座大楼很可能被用于运载火箭，而不是土星五号。事实上，它在1981年至2011年为航天飞机提供服务。返回地球后，航天飞机将在每次任务后滚入VAB进行翻新。
九年前航天飞机退役后，这座洞穴般的建筑空置了一段时间。但正如德布斯在1965年指出的那样，这座建筑是为了服务于国家的大型火箭而建造的，很快就会再次被召唤。VAB有四个高位海湾，在未来十年内将作各种用途。其中一枚将用于堆叠NASA的大型太空发射系统火箭，另一枚目前正用于诺斯罗普·格鲁曼公司的欧米茄火箭。
这两个大型火箭的未来还远未确定--只要政府买单(美国国家航空航天局(NASA)为SLS火箭买单，美国太空部队(US Space Force)为欧米茄(Omega)买单)，每一枚火箭都可能存在-但随着火箭的来来去去，VAB保持不变。
note_en:
NASA's Kennedy Space Center is now nearly six decades old—it was formally created on July 1, 1962 as a separate entity from Marshall Space Flight Center in Alabama. Construction began soon after.
 At the time, the "Launch Operations Directorate" under Wernher von Braun and his team of German scientists was based at Marshall. But NASA's leaders realized they would need their own facilities in Florida alongside the Cape Canaveral Air Force Station. So they created a new "Launch Operations Center" on nearby Merritt Island. President Lyndon B. Johnson would rename the facility Kennedy Space Center a week after President John F. Kennedy's November 1963 assassination in Dallas.
  As plans for the Apollo Program developed, NASA also soon realized it would need a large building in which to assemble the Saturn V rocket that would power the Moon landings. Work began on what was then known as the Vertical Assembly Building (VAB), where the big rocket would be stacked in a vertical configuration before rolling out to the launch pad.
 The 160-meter-tall building was topped off in 1965 and completed in 1966. Construction photos of the VAB's development—the building required almost 90,000 metric tons of steel—are as incredible as those of the finished building itself. But NASA officials wanted to make clear at the time that this building was not an end in itself. Rather, it was a means to an end.
 "This building is not a monument,"  said Kurt Debus, the German rocket scientist who supervised construction of the Florida facilities, in 1965. "It is a tool, if you will, capable of accommodating heavy launch vehicles. So if people are impressed by its bigness, they should be mindful that bigness in this case is a factor of the rocket-powered transportation systems necessary to provide the United States with a broad capability to do whatever is the national purpose in outer space."
 Even before it was completed, the VAB's name was changed from  Vertical to Vehicle Assembly Building because it was felt that the building might well be used for launch vehicles other than the Saturn V. And indeed it would, serving the space shuttle from 1981 to 2011. After returning to Earth, the space plane would roll into the VAB for refurbishment after each mission.
 Following the shuttle's retirement nine years ago, the cavernous building sat empty for a time. But as Debus noted back in 1965, the building was built to serve the nation's large rockets and would soon be called upon again. The VAB has four high bays, which will be used for various purposes during the coming decade. One will be used to stack NASA's big Space Launch System rocket, and another is presently in use for Northrop Grumman's Omega rocket.
 The future of both these large rockets is far from certain—each is likely to only exist as long as the government foots the bill (NASA for the SLS rocket, the US Space Force for Omega)—but as rockets come and go, the VAB remains the same.
title: NASA最具标志性的建筑已有55年历史，刚刚开始
title_en: NASA’s most iconic building is 55 years old and just getting started
